subject: ap-psychology
title: Motivation, Personality, and Social Psychology — Concept Application

parts.prompt: Explain how Soundwave amplitude relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that relevant to the scenario, a loud sound is associated with high  soundwaves, or a soft sound is associated with low soundwaves.

required_elements: The height of the soundwaves that were created when the lamp crashed must have been very high because Steve referred to it as loud. The sound of the lamp crashing was so loud because it had a high amplitude. Steve did not hear the timer chime because it has low soundwaves, and he almost burned the eggs.
alternatives.correct_example: The height of the soundwaves that were created when the lamp crashed must have been very high because Steve referred to it as loud. The sound of the lamp crashing was so loud because it had a high amplitude. Steve did not hear the timer chime because it has low soundwaves, and he almost burned the eggs.

scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate that relevant to the scenario, a loud sound is associated with high soundwaves, or a soft sound is associated with low soundwaves.

sample_responses.response: The height of the soundwaves that were created when the lamp crashed must have been very high because Steve referred to it as loud.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — connects the loud lamp crash to high soundwaves (amplitude).

sample_responses.response: The sound of the lamp crashing was so loud because it had a high amplitude.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — directly identifies the loud crash as a high-amplitude sound.

sample_responses.response: Steve did not hear the timer chime because it has low soundwaves, and he almost burned the eggs.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — applies the soft-sound / low-amplitude direction of the relationship.

sample_responses.response: The crash of the lamp had a high pitch.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — confuses amplitude (loudness) with pitch (frequency).

parts.prompt: Explain how Procedural memory relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that Steve and/or the children remember how to physically do  something in the scenario (e.g., making breakfast) without paying conscious attention (e.g.,  automatically, unconsciously, muscle memory, nondeclarative, well-practiced/learned) to  doing so.

required_elements: The children have set the breakfast table so much that they do it automatically.
alternatives.correct_example: The children have set the breakfast table so much that they do it automatically.

scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate that Steve and/or the children remember how to physically do something in the scenario (e.g., making breakfast) without paying conscious attention (e.g., automatically, unconsciously, muscle memory, nondeclarative, well-practiced/learned) to doing so.

sample_responses.response: The children have set the breakfast table so much that they do it automatically.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — a well-practiced motor routine (setting the table) performed automatically.

sample_responses.response: Steve remembered that the breakfast was cooking, so he had to go in and keep it from burning.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — this is declarative memory (remembering a fact), not procedural memory.

sample_responses.response: The kids know they are supposed to take out the plates and then open the cupboard to find juice glasses.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — describes a conscious step-by-step sequence, not an automatic motor skill.

sample_responses.response: Steve remembers where the couch is located in the living room so that he does not trip on it as he walks toward the broken lamp.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — remembering a spatial location is not a physical/motor action performed automatically.

parts.prompt: Explain how Diffusion of responsibility relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that the children do not attempt to clean up the broken lamp  because they assume others are likely to do it.

required_elements: The children are not cleaning up the lamp because they think someone else will do it. The younger children assume the older children will clean up the lamp.
alternatives.correct_example: The children are not cleaning up the lamp because they think someone else will do it. The younger children assume the older children will clean up the lamp.

scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate that the children do not attempt to clean up the broken lamp because they assume others are likely to do it.

sample_responses.response: The children are not cleaning up the lamp because they think someone else will do it.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — directly captures the assumption that another bystander will act.

sample_responses.response: The younger children assume the older children will clean up the lamp.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — identifies a specific subgroup assumed to take responsibility.

sample_responses.response: The children don't clean up the lamp because they are not responsible for their actions.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — describes irresponsibility as a trait, not diffusion of responsibility.

sample_responses.response: The children are watching TV, so they don't clean up the lamp.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — attributes inaction to distraction, not to the assumption that others will act.

sample_responses.response: The children want to wait until their show is over before they clean up the lamp.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — frames inaction as deliberate delay, not as deferral to other bystanders.

sample_responses.response: The dad thinks that all the children are responsible for cleaning up the lamp.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — describes the father assigning responsibility, not the children's bystander assumption.

parts.prompt: Explain how Dispositional attribution relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that Steve and/or the children overestimated the internal factors  influencing their own or someone else's behavior in the scenario.

required_elements: Steve decided that his children were clumsy and irresponsible. Steve concludes that he is a mean person for disciplining his children.
alternatives.correct_example: Steve decided that his children were clumsy and irresponsible. Steve concludes that he is a mean person for disciplining his children.

scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate that Steve and/or the children overestimated the internal factors influencing their own or someone else's behavior in the scenario.

sample_responses.response: Steve decided that his children were clumsy and irresponsible.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — Steve attributes the children's behavior to internal character traits.

sample_responses.response: Steve concludes that he is a mean person for disciplining his children.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — Steve makes an internal attribution about himself.

sample_responses.response: Steve thought his children were probably just distracted by the TV when the lamp broke.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — this is a situational attribution (external distraction), the opposite of dispositional.

parts.prompt: Explain how Ghrelin relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that for someone in the scenario, ghrelin levels are high when  they are hungry or low when they are not hungry.

required_elements: While the children waited for breakfast, ghrelin caused feelings of hunger.
alternatives.correct_example: While the children waited for breakfast, ghrelin caused feelings of hunger.

required_elements: Steve was able to lower his ghrelin levels because he ate all the breakfast.
alternatives.correct_example: Steve was able to lower his ghrelin levels because he ate all the breakfast.

scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate that for someone in the scenario, ghrelin levels are high when they are hungry or low when they are not hungry.

sample_responses.response: While the children waited for breakfast, ghrelin caused feelings of hunger.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — applies ghrelin to cause of hunger while waiting for food.

sample_responses.response: Steve was able to lower his ghrelin levels because he ate all the breakfast.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — correctly shows eating reducing ghrelin.

sample_responses.response: The children's ghrelin levels were lower because they were hungry.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — reverses the relationship; hunger is associated with HIGH ghrelin.

parts.prompt: Instead of asking the children what happened, Steve immediately turns off the television and sends them all outside to rake the large amount of leaves in the backyard. Steve stays irritable for the rest of the day. Explain how Authoritarian parenting style relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that Steve is demonstrating a parenting style that includes  high control, no discussion of expectations or consequences (one-way  communication), or that focuses on obedience and discipline.

required_elements: Steve's parenting style most closely fits authoritarian because he was quick to punish them for the mistake they made (breaking the lamp) by making them stop playing to rake leaves. Steve was more interested in them following rules than understanding what happened.
alternatives.correct_example: Steve's parenting style most closely fits authoritarian because he was quick to punish them for the mistake they made (breaking the lamp) by making them stop playing to rake leaves. Steve was more interested in them following rules than understanding what happened.

scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate that Steve is demonstrating a parenting style that includes high control, no discussion of expectations or consequences (one-way communication), or that focuses on obedience and discipline.

sample_responses.response: Steve's parenting style most closely fits authoritarian because he was quick to punish them for the mistake they made (breaking the lamp) by making them stop playing to rake leaves.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — high control and swift punishment without dialogue.

sample_responses.response: Steve was more interested in them following rules than understanding what happened.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — emphasis on obedience over discussion exemplifies authoritarian parenting.

sample_responses.response: Steve explained to the children why he punished them.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — explanation and two-way communication are features of authoritative, not authoritarian, parenting.

parts.prompt: Instead of asking the children what happened, Steve immediately turns off the television and sends them all outside to rake the large amount of leaves in the backyard. Steve stays irritable for the rest of the day. Explain how Steve's high level of the Big Five trait of neuroticism relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that an enduring characteristic of neuroticism (e.g., depression, negative feelings, anxiety) explains Steve's behavior in the scenario.

required_elements: Steve remains irritable all day and doesn't seem to recover from this minor event, indicating a lack of stability and high level of neuroticism. Steve experiences an extreme, negative emotional change throughout the scenario, showing that he is high in neuroticism. Steve gets overly upset at the sound of the lamp breaking.
alternatives.correct_example: Steve remains irritable all day and doesn't seem to recover from this minor event, indicating a lack of stability and high level of neuroticism. Steve experiences an extreme, negative emotional change throughout the scenario, showing that he is high in neuroticism. Steve gets overly upset at the sound of the lamp breaking.

scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate that an enduring characteristic of neuroticism (e.g., depression, negative feelings, anxiety) explains Steve's behavior in the scenario.

sample_responses.response: Steve remains irritable all day and doesn't seem to recover from this minor event, indicating a lack of stability and high level of neuroticism.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — ties persistent irritability to enduring emotional instability.

sample_responses.response: Steve experiences an extreme, negative emotional change throughout the scenario, showing that he is high in neuroticism.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — links negative emotional reactivity to the trait of neuroticism.

sample_responses.response: Steve gets overly upset at the sound of the lamp breaking.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — a disproportionate negative emotional reaction illustrates high neuroticism.

sample_responses.response: There is evidence that Steve is high in neuroticism because he stays irritable the rest of the day.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — restates observable behavior without explaining what neuroticism is.

sample_responses.response: Steve is neurotic about his kids and their safety.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — uses 'neurotic' colloquially rather than in the Big Five sense.

sample_responses.response: Steve is unhappy that the lamp broke.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — describes a momentary reaction without connecting it to an enduring trait.
